title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a sports engineer called Ryan Miller talking about how technology is changing professional athletics. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a sports engineer called Ryan Miller talking about how technology is changing professional athletics. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ryan Miller discusses the impact of advanced materials and data analysis on athlete performance.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Ryan. In elite sports, the difference between a gold medal and fourth place is often less than a hundredth of a second. My job is to find that tiny margin. I’m a sports engineer. My interest in this field didn't start with a physics degree; it actually began when I modified my own [7]bicycle frame[/7] as a teenager to make it more aerodynamic. I was obsessed with how small changes in shape could lead to massive increases in speed.

Today, we work with materials that were originally designed for the aerospace industry. Carbon fiber is everywhere, but it’s the way we layer it that matters. I’ve found that the [8]torsional stiffness[/8] of a shoe or a racket is the most important mechanical factor we measure. It determines how much energy is returned to the athlete during a movement. If it’s too flexible, energy is wasted; if it’s too stiff, it can cause injury. It’s a delicate balance.

We also use 'computational fluid dynamics' to study how air moves around an athlete. We used to rely solely on wind tunnels, but now we can run thousands of simulations on a computer. I noticed that the [9]seam placement[/9] on an athlete’s suit can actually reduce drag by up to 5%. It sounds insignificant, but in a 100-meter sprint, it’s the difference between breaking a record and not even qualifying. Every millimeter of fabric is calculated.

Data is the new frontier. Athletes now wear sensors that track everything from heart rate to muscle fatigue. I’ve found that monitoring [10]hydration levels[/10] in real-time is the most valuable insight we can provide a coach. It allows them to know exactly when a player needs to be substituted before their performance actually begins to drop. It’s about being proactive rather than reactive.

However, technology is also creating an 'equipment gap'. I’m worried that the [11]high cost[/11] of these advanced tools is making it impossible for athletes from developing nations to compete on a level playing field. If you don't have access to a million-dollar lab, are you already at a disadvantage? It’s an ethical question that the sporting world needs to address.

We are also seeing a rise in 'smart' equipment. I recently worked on a project involving a [12]sensored cricket bat[/12] that gives instant feedback on the angle of the strike. It’s an incredible tool for training, as it allows players to correct their technique in seconds rather than waiting to watch video footage later that day. It’s making coaching much more efficient.

Looking forward, I believe that 'biometric integration' is the next big step. I noticed that [13]3D-printed prosthetics[/13] are already becoming so advanced that they may soon outperform natural limbs in specific tasks. This will lead to fascinating, and perhaps difficult, conversations about what it means to be a 'natural' athlete versus a 'human-machine' hybrid.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]safety protocol[/14] for the use of AI in player recruitment. We want to ensure that algorithms aren't overlooking talent just because an athlete doesn't fit a specific data profile. Thanks for listening.
